cataloging_subject.label: The Siege of Jerusalem
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English alliterative poem conventionally lineated to 1,334 lines. The work is distinct from its nine surviving manuscript witnesses, the Kölbing-Day edition, Hoyt N. Duggan's electronic transcription, the incomplete University of Michigan XML representation, and the separate rhyming Destruction of Jerusalem tradition.

catalog_summary.title: The Siege of Jerusalem
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.editor: Eugen Kölbing and Mabel Day; electronic transcription by Hoyt N. Duggan
catalog_summary.date: Probably the 1370s or 1380s; copied in nine surviving witnesses from the late fourteenth through the fifteenth century
catalog_summary.region: West Riding of Yorkshire, near Barnoldswick and Earby (authorial-dialect localization; composition place not securely established)

titles.supplied_by: Rubric in British Library, Add MS 31042 (witness A)
notes: This manuscript rubric is title evidence for one witness, not proof of an authorial title.

titles.value: In Tiberius time the true emperor
titles.supplied_by: Normalized from the poem's opening line

agents.name: Eugen Kölbing
notes: Began the EETS edition; Mabel Day completed and revised it after his death.

scope.description: The 1932 EETS source edition underlying Duggan's transcription
Completion and revision of the 1932 EETS source edition
The electronic transcription represented by OTA 1357 and, incompletely, by the U-M/General Corpus XML

assertion.notes: Modern scholarship favors the 1370s or 1380s; the older c. 1390-1400 date retained in the U-M XML header is not treated as settled.

date_statements.display: Late fourteenth century, represented jointly by witnesses L and P under specialist dating

assertion.notes: Specialist descriptions assign both L and P to the late fourteenth century, but their exact priority is not established here. The current Bodleian catalog instead dates the L codex to the beginning of the fifteenth century; the disagreement is preserved in lineage.

date_statements.display: Roman-Jewish war and destruction of Jerusalem, conventionally 66-73 CE and centered on the siege of 70 CE

places.label: Barnoldswick-Earby area, West Riding of Yorkshire
notes: A linguistic localization of the poem's authorial dialect, not a confirmed place of composition or a statement about every surviving scribe.

assertion.notes: The localization is a scholarly reconstruction from authorial-language features.

subject_statements.label: Christian anti-Judaism, antisemitism, and anti-Jewish violence
notes: This heading documents harmful content for responsible discovery; it does not endorse the poem's collective blame, supersessionism, dehumanization, or violence against Jews.

subject_statements.label: Divine vengeance, punishment, and Christian supersession
notes: The statement describes the poem's ideology rather than accepting its theology or historical claims.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English alliterative romance recasts the Roman siege and destruction of Jerusalem as Christian vengeance for Christ's death. It joins the healing and conversion of Titus and Vespasian to a long military narrative of factional conflict, famine, cannibalism, mass death, enslavement, and the destruction of the Temple. The poem repeatedly blames Jews collectively and uses graphic anti-Jewish violence, dehumanization, and supersessionist theology; those features are documented here for responsible discovery and study, not repeated as historical truth or endorsed.

The complete poem is conventionally lineated to 1,334 lines. The local SJerus XML contains 1,234 L elements with printed line labels 1-997 and 1098-1334; exactly lines 998-1097 are absent. Its thirteen DIV1 wrappers are supplied hundred-line navigation groups rather than intellectual sections. The current OTA deposit also preserves Duggan's complete 1-1334 working transcription, so the gap belongs to the U-M XML representation, not to the abstract work.

Nine manuscript witnesses are currently known: A, C, D, E, Ex, L, P, U, and V. Kölbing and Day knew seven and used L as copy-text; P and the Ex binding fragment became known after their edition. The witness tradition and exact survival gaps belong in the linked lineage manifest and must not be flattened into a claim that all witnesses are complete or that the edition used all nine.

extent.description: The local XML contains one partial electronic representation of a conventionally 1,334-line poem: 1,234 encoded verse lines in 13 supplied navigation divisions, with line labels 1-997 and 1098-1334. It records 71 printed-page breaks and omits exactly 100 conventional line positions, 998-1097.

content_parts.label: The Siege of Jerusalem
content_parts.summary: The whole alliterative narrative from the opening in Tiberius's reign through the Roman return after Jerusalem's destruction; the local XML lacks conventional lines 998-1097.
notes: The thirteen XML divisions are not cataloged as thirteen work sections.
The metrics describe the local representation; the complete work's conventional extent is 1,334 lines.

related_works.title: Vindicta Salvatoris
notes: A principal source tradition for approximately the opening 200 lines. No exact physical exemplar is identified here.

related_works.title: Bible en français by Roger d'Argenteuil
notes: Modern source analysis associates this tradition especially with lines 201-264 and 325-788; the physical exemplar remains unidentified.

related_works.title: Polychronicon
notes: Ranulf Higden's chronicle is a major source from about line 789 onward, but no exact manuscript exemplar is established.

related_works.title: Legenda aurea
notes: A source for a passage around conventional lines 1022-1062; this descriptive relation does not claim an identified historical source copy.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_from_OTA/SJerus.xml, HEADER and BODY.
evidence.summary: The XML names the anonymous work, 1993 U-M distribution, OTA identifier U*-1357-A, Kölbing and Day's 1932 EETS edition, Duggan's unproofread manuscript emendation, and the omitted front matter, back matter, notes, and illustrations.

evidence.citation: Structural and line-label inspection of the checked-in SJerus XML, performed 2026-07-15.
evidence.summary: Inspection found 1,234 L, 13 supplied-line-group DIV1, 71 PB, and 14 HEAD elements. Printed line labels cover 1-997 and 1098-1334; exactly 998-1097 are absent, while XML line IDs remain sequential l.1-l.1234.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive / University of Oxford, The siege of Jerusalem, handle 20.500.14106/1357.
evidence.locator: Item record and three public text bitstreams
evidence.summary: The current record identifies Hoyt N. Duggan as compiler and provides three public text files. Direct inspection shows one complete working transcription covering conventional lines 1-1334, one file for 1-200, and one for 601-997 plus 1098-1334.

evidence.citation: Poems of the Middle English Penitential Tradition / PCMEP, The Siege of Jerusalem, text details.
evidence.locator: Date, dialect, genre, sources, extent, and manuscripts
evidence.summary: The specialist repertory records the anonymous 1,334-line alliterative poem, late-fourteenth-century date, West Riding localization, nine witnesses, alternate titles, genres, and composite literary sources.

evidence.citation: Michael Livingston, ed., The Siege of Jerusalem, Middle English Texts Series, Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2004.
evidence.locator: Introduction, text, explanatory notes, and bibliography
evidence.summary: The modern scholarly edition supports the work's date, dialect localization, alliterative form, romance and Vengeance-of-Our-Lord classifications, subject description, source analysis, nine-witness tradition, and modern stemma.
notes: The PDF opens and extracts successfully. qpdf reports recoverable shared-object and offset warnings; no repair was applied to the cached bytes.

evidence.citation: Timothy L. Stinson, 'Makeres of the Mind: Authorial Intention, Editorial Practice, and The Siege of Jerusalem,' Yearbook of Langland Studies 25 (2011): 39-62, doi:10.1484/J.YLS.1.102108.
evidence.locator: pp. 41-43, witness list and comparison of the Kölbing-Day and Hanna-Lawton editions
evidence.summary: Stinson lists all nine witnesses, explains that P and Ex became known only after the Kölbing-Day edition, and identifies L as that edition's copy-text while characterizing its emendation as light relative to Hanna-Lawton.
notes: The current NCSU URL returns 566,433 bytes identical to the cached PDF. The 24-page file opens and extracts successfully; qpdf reports recoverable shared-object and linearization hint-table warnings, and no repair was applied.
Neither the PDF nor the current NCSU download host supplies a source-specific open reuse license.

evidence.citation: Timothy L. Stinson, ed., Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive, overview and manuscript descriptions.
evidence.summary: The scholarly archive identifies all nine surviving witnesses and offers full diplomatic transcriptions, descriptions, and view-only images for their surviving work leaves.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog, Eugen Kölbing and Mabel Day, The Siege of Jerusalem, EETS OS 188, 1932, record 34776.
evidence.summary: The record gives the edition's title, editors, EETS series and publication facts, extent, and Day's completion and revision of Kölbing's unfinished edition.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Laud Misc. 656 and The siege of Jerusalem, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries.
evidence.summary: The official catalog identifies the poem in Laud Misc. 656 and dates the parchment codex to the beginning of the fifteenth century; the linked specialist archive assigns the target work to fols. 1v-19r and a late-fourteenth-century date.
notes: The cached XML is the Bodleian catalog's source record at pinned commit e865840e73308c58846a24da10c431a88193ddd2; current and specialist date statements are preserved separately in lineage.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition be dated or localized more narrowly without converting the inferred authorial dialect or the proposed Bolton Abbey association into a fact?
next_steps: Review recent linguistic and historical scholarship that postdates the general PCMEP and 2004 METS summaries.
Record competing chronologies as separate attributed assertions if they materially differ from the 1370s-1380s proposal.

notes: The U-M header's c. 1390-1400 creation date is retained as source history but not promoted over later scholarship favoring the 1370s or 1380s.
The Kölbing-Day subtitle all other extant MSS refers to the seven witnesses known to those editors, not all nine manuscripts known today.
Rights and access for medieval text, printed editions, electronic encodings, transcriptions, and manuscript images are separated in the lineage manifest.
